content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 23, 1998

Bruce Leavitt
Assistant City Attorney
City of Santa Rosa
100 Santa Rosa Avenue
Post Office Box 1678
Santa Rosa, California 95402-1678

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-98-161

Dear Mr. Leavitt:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Dr. Stephanie Nacouzi
regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please bear in mind that
nothing in this letter should be construed as evaluation of any conduct which may already have
taken place. Further, this letter is based on the facts you have presented to us. The Commission
does not act as finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

May Dr. Nacouzi participate in decisions concerning establishment of the McDonald
Historic Preservation District?

CONCLUSION

Dr. Nacouzi may participate in decisions concerning establishment of the historic
preservation district so long as she is able to determine that the decision will not change the
legally permissible uses of the property she leases.

___________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-98-161
Page 2

FACTS

Dr. Stephanie Nacouzi is a newly appointed member of the Santa Rosa Cultural Heritage
Board (the "board"). The board will soon consider whether a preservation district, the McDonald
Historic Preservation District, should be established for an area around McDonald Avenue and
adjacent streets, a historic residential area in the City of Santa Rosa. Dr. Nacouzi leases medical
office space at 95 Montgomery Drive which is more than 300 feet, but less than 2,500 feet, from
the boundaries of the proposed preservation district.

Under local law, the owner of property within a preservation district is required to
maintain the exterior of any structure in good repair. Good repair is defined as that level of
maintenance and repair which clearly insures the continued availability of the structures for
reasonable uses while preventing deterioration, dilapidation and decay. A property owner may
not undertake demolition, restoration, alteration or other changes to the exterior appearance of
any structure within a preservation district without a permit from the board. There are exceptions
in cases of economic hardship or situations involving dangerous buildings.

A preservation district may be established after a proposal is studied by the board and a
public hearing is held. The board then makes a recommendation to the city council, which may
decide to establish the district, possibly with modified boundaries, or to reject the proposal. The
board itself may deny a proposal, which becomes the final decision on the matter unless the
denial is appealed to the city council.

Dr. Nacouzi has advised you that she sees no relationship between the formation of the
preservation district and future rent for her medical office. She has also advised you that she
would not change the use of this property whether or not the district is formed. The office, aside
from containing adequate medical office facilities, is leased primarily for its convenience and
proximity to Memorial Hospital and other medical facilities and services in the area. Whether or
not the district is formed, she does not foresee any resulting changes in the use of properties
within 300 feet of her medical office, nor a change in the termination date of her lease.
Dr. Nacouzi's opinions are based, at least in part, on opinions to the same effect by the city's
Director of Community Development and the city's Right of Way Agent, who is a licensed real
property appraiser.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) A "public official" is defined as a
member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency (Section 82048;
Regulation 18700) and, as you have recognized, Dr. Nacouzi is a "public official" under the Act
who would be making a governmental decision by voting on the preservation district.

File No. A-98-161
Page 3

Section 87103 provides that an official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member
of his or her immediate family, or on a variety of economic interests, including:

"(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a
direct or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000)
or more.
(b) Any real property in which the public official has a
direct or indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than
loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position
of management."

Dr. Nacouzi's question focuses specifically on subdivision (b), on potential conflicts of
interest relating to her leasehold interest in the medical office building. Section 82033 provides
that an "interest in real property" includes any leasehold interest owned directly, indirectly or
beneficially by the public official, if the fair market value of the interest is $1,000 or more.
Regulation 18729 provides that the value of a leasehold interest is the amount of rent payable
during a 12 month period. For purposes of this letter we presume that this amount is $1,000 or
more, and that Dr. Nacouzi (rather than a professional corporation) is the lessee. Dr. Nacouzi's
leasehold interest therefore qualifies as a financial interest under Section 87103(b), if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect upon it.

Materiality of any effect is determined by standards that depend, in the first instance, on
whether the financial interest would be directly or indirectly affected by a decision. Dr.
Nacouzi's leasehold interest is not directly involved in the decision on establishment of the
preservation district. Regulation 18702.1(a)(3) specifies certain governmental decisions that are
presumed to directly involve real property interests, but since the medical office building would
be outside the boundaries of the preservation district, it does not appear that such a decision is

File No. A-98-161
Page 4

described within this subdivision. Moreover, leasehold interests are expressly excluded from the
scope of subdivision (a)(3). (Dunsford Advice Letter, No. A-97-369.)²

If a decision does not directly involve a real property interest, the materiality thresholds
of Regulation 18702.4 apply to determine whether a decision may have an indirect effect.
(Payton Advice Letter, No. A-95-397.) Under this regulation, the effect of a decision is material
as to a leasehold interest in real property if any of the following apply:

"(a) The decision will change the legally allowable use of
the leased property, and the lessee has a right to sublease the
property;
(b) It is reasonably foreseeable that the lessee will change
the actual use of the property as a result of the decision;
(c) It is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will result
in a change in the actual use of property within 300 feet of the
leased property, and the changed use will significantly enhance or
significantly decrease the use or enjoyment of the leased property;
(d) The decision will increase or decrease the amount of
rent for the leased property by $250 or 5 percent, whichever is
greater, during any 12-month period following the decision; or
(e) The decision will result in a change in the termination
date of the lease."

Referring to subdivision (a), you have not told us whether the decision would change the
legally allowable use of the leasehold, or whether (as is normally the case) the lessee has a right
to sublease the premises. If the answer to both of these questions is yes, then the decision would
have a material financial effect on Dr. Nacouzi's financial interest in the lease, presenting a
disqualifying conflict of interest in the decision on establishment of the preservation district.

If not disqualified under Regulation 18702.4(a), it appears that Dr. Nacouzi will not have
a conflict of interest in this decision, since she has addressed and dismissed the remaining
threshold effects. Dr. Nacouzi has stated that the decision will not result in a change in the
termination date of her lease, and that she will not change her use of the leasehold as a result of
the decision. So long as it is reasonable for Dr. Nacouzi to rely on the opinions of the city's

_______________________

² The Hargrave Advice Letter, No. A-97-170, treated a leasehold interest as "directly" affected under
Regulation 18702.1(a)(3) but, as later shown in the Dunsford Advice Letter, historical analysis establishes that
leasehold interests were meant to be excluded from any consideration under Regulation 18702.1(a)(3). The
Hargrave Advice Letter is hereby superseded on this point.

File No. A-98-161
Page 5

Director of Community Development and its Right of Way Agent, effects described in
subdivisions (c) and (d) would also be "unforeseeable" for purposes of conflicts analysis.³

If you have any other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:LTW:tls

___________________________
³ The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

legally permissible uses of the property she leases.Page 2

Historic Preservation District?

CONCLUSION: Dr. Nacouzi may participate in decisions concerning establishment of the historic
preservation district so long as she is able to determine that the decision will not change the
legally permissible uses of the property she leases.Page 2
